name: No Child Left Behind
description: No Child Left Behind (NCLB), enacted in 2002, was a United States federal law that mandated standardized testing and accountability measures for public schools. Proponents argued that it aimed to close achievement gaps between different student groups, particularly those from disadvantaged backgrounds, and improve overall educational quality. The law required states to develop assessments in basic skills to be given to all students in certain grades. Schools failing to meet adequate yearly progress (AYP) benchmarks for student performance faced sanctions. Some alternative interpretations view NCLB as a deliberate attempt to undermine public education, potentially benefiting private and charter schools. Evidence cited includes the increased emphasis on standardized testing, which critics argue narrowed the curriculum and 'taught to the test,' neglecting other important areas of learning. Further, some argue that the sanctions imposed on failing schools disproportionately impacted low-income and minority communities, exacerbating existing inequalities. Mainstream scholarly perspectives generally acknowledge that NCLB increased accountability but also highlight its unintended consequences. These include the narrowing of the curriculum, increased pressure on teachers, and the potential for schools to 'game the system' to meet AYP targets. Many studies suggest that NCLB's impact on overall student achievement was limited, and that the law may have even widened achievement gaps in some areas. The law was eventually replaced by the Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA) in 2015.
subtitle: Federal education reform and its controversies

claims.evidence: Increased standardized testing provided data on student performance and school effectiveness.
claims.criticism: Accountability measures were often based solely on standardized test scores, leading to a narrow view of educational success.

claims.claim: NCLB closed achievement gaps between student groups.
claims.evidence: The law aimed to ensure all students, regardless of background, met proficiency standards.
claims.criticism: Studies suggest NCLB had limited success in closing achievement gaps and may have even widened them in some areas.

claims.claim: NCLB was a deliberate attempt to privatize education.
claims.evidence: The law's focus on standardized testing and sanctions for failing schools created opportunities for charter and private schools to gain market share.
claims.criticism: While NCLB may have indirectly benefited some private schools, there is no direct evidence that this was its primary intention. The law also aimed to improve public schools.

keyFigures.name: George W. Bush
keyFigures.role: President who signed NCLB into law

keyFigures.name: Diane Ravitch
keyFigures.role: Education historian and critic of NCLB

mythologyConnections: The concept of a 'level playing field' resonates with myths about social justice and fairness.
The idea of 'leaving no child behind' can be seen as a modern iteration of utopian ideals found in various mythologies.

timeline.event: No Child Left Behind Act is passed by Congress.
NCLB is signed into law by President George W. Bush.
Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA) replaces NCLB.
